title: Stop, thoughts of sensual desire! Stop, thoughts of ill-will! Stop, thoughts of harmfulness! Enough, thoughts of sensual desire! Enough, thoughts of ill-will! Enough, thoughts of…
content:
Stop, thoughts of sensual desire!
Stop, thoughts of ill-will!
Stop, thoughts of harmfulness!
Enough, thoughts of sensual desire!
Enough, thoughts of ill-will!
Enough, thoughts of harmfulness!

title: “Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having…
content: “Suppose, monks, a fisherman throws a baited hook into a deep lake. A fish, seeing the bait, swallows the hook. Thus, monks, that fish, having swallowed the fisherman’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the fisherman’s will. So too, monks, there are these six hooks in the world, leading to the misfortune of beings, to the calamity of living beings. What six? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will. There are sounds … odors … tastes … touches … phenomena cognizable by the mind that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those phenomena, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk is called one who has swallowed Māra’s hook, has fallen into misfortune, into calamity, subject to the Evil One’s will.”

title: - What gives birth to a person? What always runs about? What undergoes rebirth? What does a person fear? - Craving gives birth to a…
content:
- What gives birth to a person?
What always runs about?
What undergoes rebirth?
What does a person fear?
- Craving gives birth to a person,
The mind itself always runs about,
Beings undergo rebirth,
Suffering is what a person fears.

title: “Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks,…
content: “Monks, what are the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint? Here, a monk, reflecting wisely, lives with the restraint of the eye faculty. Monks, if he lives without restraining the eye faculty, harmful, feverish defilements might arise. If he restrains the eye faculty, those harmful, feverish defilements are no more. These, monks, are called the defilements that should be abandoned by restraint.”

title: Then the Blessed One, knowing that it was Māra the Evil One, recited this verse to Māra: Whatever forms there are, In this world or…
content:
Then the Blessed One, knowing that it was Māra the Evil One, recited this verse to Māra:

Whatever forms there are,
In this world or the next,
With excellent colors,
Shining brightly in the sky —
All those forms,
Praised by Māra,
Are just bait cast out
To destroy the fish.

title: “Monks, when a monk has abandoned ignorance and aroused true knowledge, then with the abandonment of ignorance and the arising of true knowledge, he does…
content: “Monks, when a monk has abandoned ignorance and aroused true knowledge, then with the abandonment of ignorance and the arising of true knowledge, he does not form any volitional formation, does not generate any kamma. Without forming, without generating, he does not cling to anything in the world. Without clinging, he is not agitated. Without agitation, he personally attains Nibbāna. He knows: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”

title: “‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary…
content: “‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary person does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination’ as form is subject to origination; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to cessation’ as form is subject to cessation; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination and cessation’ as form is subject to origination and cessation. This, monk, is called ignorance. And to that extent one is called ignorant.”

title: “Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best…
content: “Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best of these two pleasures, monks, is the pleasure of renunciation.”

title: “And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind;…
content: “And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind; anger is a defilement of the mind; hostility is a defilement of the mind; hypocrisy, spite, envy, stinginess, deceit, fraud, obstinacy, impetuosity, conceit, arrogance, vanity, heedlessness are defilements of the mind.”

title: “One who once was heedless and later is not, Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.” “He who deeds of merit makes, And…
content:
“One who once was heedless and later is not,
Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.”

“He who deeds of merit makes,
And thereby his evil deeds dispels,
Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.”

“Blinded is this world,
Few are those who see clearly.
Like birds escaped from a net,
Few go to heaven.”

“Like a swan flying through the sky,
With psychic power soaring through space,
Having conquered Māra and his host,
The wise one leaves this world behind.”
